verses.commentary: God tested Abraham. This is explicit. Not temptation to sin but testing of faith. Some time later, after Isaac has grown. Abraham! The direct address. Here I am, a response of availability and readiness.

The command builds. Your son. Your only son (unique, beloved). Whom you love. Isaac, finally named. Each phrase increases the pain. Go to Moriah. Sacrifice him, literally cause him to go up, a burnt offering. Total sacrifice, consumed by fire. On a mountain I will show you. The destination is unspecified, echoing the original call (12:1). This is the supreme test.

Early next morning. No delay, no debate. Loaded his donkey. Took servants and Isaac. Cut wood for the burnt offering. Abraham prepares meticulously. He set out. Obedience without recorded objection. The silence is deafening.
Third day, significant number. He saw the place. The journey gives time for anguish and reflection. Yet Abraham continues.

Stay here. I and the boy, only the two of them. We will worship. We will come back to you. Abraham's faith speaks. Hebrews 11:19 says he believed God could raise Isaac from the dead. Or is this deception to prevent interference? The text leaves it ambiguous.

Isaac carries the wood for his own sacrifice. The fire and the knife Abraham carries. As the two of them went on together. The repetition emphasizes the shared journey, the father and son walking toward death.
Isaac's question. Father. Yes, my son. Tender address. Isaac notices. The wood and fire are here. But where is the lamb? The question pierces. Isaac is old enough to understand sacrifice.

Abraham's answer. God himself will provide, God will see, God will see to it. The lamb. My son. Is this faith or evasion? Prophetic or tragic? The phrase God will provide becomes the mountain's name (verse 14). And the two of them went on together, repeated. The intimacy deepens the horror and the trust.

They reached the place. Abraham built an altar. Arranged the wood. He bound Isaac. This word gives the Jewish name for this event: the Akedah, the binding. Isaac is a young man (possibly a teenager or older) who could resist. He submits. Laid him on the altar, on top of the wood. The son becomes the sacrifice.
Reached out his hand. Took the knife. To slay his son. The moment of crisis. Abraham raises the knife. The test reaches its climax.
But. The intervention. The angel calls from heaven. Abraham! Abraham! The double call shows urgency. Here I am, again the response of readiness.

Stop. Do not lay a hand. Do not do anything. The command is clear. Now I know. God's testing reveals what God already knows to Abraham and to us. You fear God. Fear means reverence, awe, obedience. You have not withheld. Your son, your only son. The test is passed. Abraham proved willing to give up everything.

Abraham looked up. A ram appears. Caught by its horns in a thicket. Divine provision. He took the ram and sacrificed it instead of his son. Substitutionary sacrifice. This foreshadows Christ, the Lamb of God (John 1:29). The ram dies in Isaac's place.

Abraham names the place. The LORD Will Provide. To this day, the narrator's time. It is said. On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided. Moriah tradition identifies this as the Temple Mount in Jerusalem, where sacrifices were later offered and where Christ died. Provision and sacrifice converge.
Second call from heaven. More blessing follows the test.
God swears. By myself, because there is no one greater (Hebrews 6:13). Declares the LORD. Because you have done this. Not withheld. The reason for blessing is Abraham's obedience.

I will surely bless, intensive form. Numerous as the stars and as the sand. Both celestial and terrestrial images. Take possession of the cities of their enemies. Victory promised. Conquest foreshadowed.

All nations will be blessed. The Abrahamic covenant reaches its fullest statement. Through your offspring (seed, singular) pointing to Christ (Galatians 3:16). Because you have obeyed. Obedience brings blessing, but the blessing is ultimately grace, not earned.
Abraham returns. Isaac is not mentioned. Jewish tradition wonders if Isaac went elsewhere. They return to Beersheba. Life continues after the test.
Transition. News from Abraham's family in Mesopotamia. Milkah, Nahor's wife (11:29). She has borne sons. The family multiplies.
Eight sons are listed. Uz, possibly Job's homeland. Aram, ancestor of Arameans.
More names. Bethuel is significant.
Rebekah is introduced. She will become Isaac's wife (chapter 24). This genealogy sets up the next generation.
Nahor's concubine also bears sons. The family continues to multiply. The focus will return to Abraham's line through Isaac and Rebekah.
